input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The physical twin system under study is a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** focused on **cyber-biophysical integration**, particularly in **automotive and industrial applications**, as described in [Document 1](https://doi.org/10.1109/MODELS58315.2023.00014). This system emphasizes **real-time data synchronization** between physical and digital domains, leveraging **cyber-physical feedback loops** for adaptive control. Key components include:
  - **Sensors and Actuators**: High-fidelity IoT-enabled devices (e.g., LiDAR, cameras, IMUs) for real-time environmental and operational data acquisition, with **latency-constrained communication** (e.g., <100 ms for critical updates).
  - **Embedded Computing**: Edge devices for localized processing to reduce latency, ensuring **deterministic timing** for real-time decision-making.
  - **Biophysical Integration**: Cross-disciplinary modeling of **human-machine interaction** (e.g., driver intent recognition) and **physical system dynamics** (e.g., vehicle dynamics, manufacturing processes).
  - **Operational Domain**: Primarily **automotive (ACC systems, autonomous driving)** and **industrial manufacturing** (e.g., assembly lines, robotics), with applications in **aerospace (space debris detection)** as referenced in [Document 16](https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0275). The system is designed to support **high-fidelity, multi-scale simulations** (e.g., from microelectronics to macro-scale processes) while adhering to **ISO 23247-1** and **ISO/IEC 30173:2023** standards for digital twin frameworks.
  The physical twin’s **key characteristics** include **real-time adaptability**, **scalability across domains**, and **interoperability** with legacy and emerging systems through standardized ontologies (e.g., for sensor data, control logic, and failure modes).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin (DT) provides the following **structured services** to users and the physical system, grounded in [Document 1](https://doi.org/10.1109/MODELS58315.2023.00014) and [Document 9](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.iotcps.2023.04.004):
  - **Monitoring**:
    - **Real-time Data Acquisition**: Continuous ingestion of sensor data (e.g., LiDAR, cameras) via **edge computing** for low-latency processing, with **DBSCAN clustering** for anomaly detection in autonomous driving applications.
    - **State Estimation**: Use of **Kalman filters** for tracking and **particle filters** for uncertainty quantification in dynamic environments.
  - **Optimization**:
    - **Adaptive Control Logic**: Dynamic reconfiguration of control algorithms (e.g., **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**) based on real-time feedback, with **physics-informed constraints** to ensure operational safety.
    - **Resource Allocation**: Offloading computationally intensive tasks (e.g., **AI-driven simulations**) to edge/cloud platforms, as described in [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).
  - **Prediction**:
    - **Fault Prediction**: Machine learning models (e.g., **support vector machines (SVMs)**) for proactive failure detection in automotive and industrial systems.
    - **Trajectory Forecasting**: Short-term predictions for autonomous vehicles using **Kalman filtering** and **recurrent neural networks (RNNs)** for path planning.
  - **Control**:
    - **Closed-Loop Regulation**: Direct actuation of physical systems via **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**-based message brokers (e.g., **MQTT/AMQP**) for interoperability.
    - **Human-in-the-Loop (HITL)**: Integration of **driver intent recognition** (e.g., gaze tracking, voice commands) into the DT for context-aware control.
  - **Visualization**:
    - **3D Interactive Dashboards**: Real-time 3D rendering of the physical twin’s state (e.g., vehicle dynamics, battery health) using **MATLAB/Simulink** for simulation validation and **ROS2 visualization tools** for IoT integration.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Operators and Developers**: Primary stakeholders for **real-time diagnostics**, **offline optimization**, and **remote management** of cyber-physical systems (as per [Document 8](https://doi.org/10.3390/asi5040065)).
    - **Industrial Engineers**: For **manufacturing process optimization** (e.g., ISO 23247-1 compliant digital twins for assembly lines).
  - **Service Capabilities**:
    - **Data-Driven Insights**: Generation of **operational metrics** (e.g., efficiency, reliability) via **SQL databases** for analytics and compliance reporting.
    - **Standardized Protocols**: Adherence to **ISO/IEC 30173:2023** for terminology consistency and **ISO 23247-1** for framework alignment.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation relies on the following **technical enablers**, as documented in [Document 1](https://doi.org/10.1109/MODELS58315.2023.00014), [Document 10](https://doi.org/), [Document 11](https://doi.org/), [Document 12](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICCWorkshops50388.2021.9473889), and [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019):
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**: Core middleware for **multi-agent coordination**, **message passing**, and **device abstraction** across heterogeneous IoT devices (e.g., sensors, actuators).
    - **MATLAB/Simulink**: Simulation engines for **modeling physics-based dynamics**, **control system design**, and **digital twin validation** (e.g., battery systems in [Document 17](https://doi.org/978-3-031-56599-1)).
    - **Python 3.8**: Scripting environment for **data processing**, **machine learning**, and **ontology management** (e.g., semantic web standards for DT data).
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **C++**: Low-level programming for **real-time embedded systems** and **high-performance computing** (e.g., edge devices).
    - **SQL Databases**: Structured storage for **historical data**, **operational metrics**, and **queryable DT state** (e.g., ISO 23247-1 compliant transaction logs).
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)**: Hybrid models combining **deep learning** with **partial differential equations (PDEs)** for high-fidelity predictions (e.g., in [Document 15](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-025-01306-0)).
    - **AI Simulation Frameworks**: Integration with **TensorFlow/PyTorch** for **end-to-end training** of DT components (e.g., autonomous driving decision-making).
  - **Standards and Frameworks**:
    - **ISO 23247-1**: Digital Twin **framework for manufacturing**, defining **overall architecture**, **data flows**, and **interoperability requirements**.
    - **ISO/IEC 30173:2023**: **Concepts and terminology** for DTs, including **ontologies**, **metadata standards**, and **semantic interoperability**.
    - **Edge Computing**: ISO 23247-compliant **distributed processing** models (e.g., [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019)) to balance **latency** and **computational resources**.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Cyber-Physical Feedback Loops**: Real-time **closed-loop control** via **ROS2 topics** (e.g., `/vehicle/state`, `/actuator/commands`) for autonomous systems.
    - **Semantic Web Technologies**: **OWL/RDF** for **ontology-driven reasoning** to link DT data with **industrial standards** (e.g., IEC 61131 for PLCs).
    - **5G/6G Enablers**: Low-latency **communication protocols** (e.g., **eMBMS**) for **high-bandwidth DT synchronization** in critical applications (e.g., aerospace [Document 16](https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0275)).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces **time synchronization with a strict 100 ms accuracy requirement** between physical and digital components, critical for safety-critical vehicular applications. This synchronization is essential for enabling computation offloading and ensuring deterministic behavior in real-time processing, where deviations beyond 100 ms would compromise system reliability and safety compliance. The temporal granularity is aligned with the hardware constraints of the underlying devices, necessitating precise clock synchronization protocols to maintain coherence across distributed entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system adopts a **multi-instance architecture with a centralized coordination framework**, where each entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User) is deployed on distinct hardware components. The **Physical Twin Entity** hosts device-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), while the **Digital Twin Entity** manages data pre-processing, actuation, and control mechanisms. The **User Entity** consolidates user-facing components into a **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** FE, splitting the traditional monolithic User Interface FE. Responsibilities are explicitly segmented by entity, with no overlap in ownership of core functionalities. The system does not explicitly mention decentralized twins or multi-instance coordination mechanisms beyond the entity-based structure defined in ISO 23247 RA, though deviations from the standard (e.g., broader Physical Twin Entity scope) imply distributed ownership models.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows an **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)**-aligned orchestration, where software components are mapped onto **Functional Entities (FEs)** and implemented as **ROS2 packages** per hardware deployment. The architecture distinguishes three primary entities:
  1. **Physical Twin Entity**: Hosts compute, sensors, actuators, and general automotive functions (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), reflecting the system’s inherent resources.
  2. **Digital Twin Entity**: Manages data management, control mechanisms, and pre-processing, diverging from ISO 23247 by consolidating OME-specific functionality into broader device-related tasks.
  3. **User Entity**: Contains the **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** components, split from the original User Interface FE. Each FE is instantiated as a ROS2 package, enabling modular execution across distributed hardware. The system lacks explicit mention of orchestration patterns (e.g., microservices, event-driven architectures) but relies on **ROS2’s publish-subscribe model** for inter-FE communication. Resource management is implicitly handled by hardware-specific FE deployment, with no mention of dynamic scaling or cloud-based orchestration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247** framework, particularly its **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**, where **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** are digitally represented for Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs) such as equipment, facilities, and processes. The **Digital Twin Entity** serves as the core model, encapsulating the OME’s digital twin, while **Functional Entities (FEs)** within it handle specialized tasks like data transformation (e.g., converting raw sensor data into human-interpretable formats) or user interfaces.

Data components are managed through **data collection and device control entities**, which interface with physical OMEs via standardized protocols (e.g., **OPC Unified Architecture**). Model relationships are implicitly defined by the **entity decomposition**—each OME’s digital twin is a cohesive unit, with FEs ensuring seamless integration of model types (e.g., physics-based simulations for process modeling). Data management is implicit in the architecture, relying on **real-time data streams** from sensors and actuators, though specific data storage or processing frameworks (e.g., databases, middleware) are not detailed in the documents.

No explicit mention of sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or data partitioning is provided, but the architecture implies **low-latency, synchronized data exchange** between physical and digital domains.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Digital Twin system includes **physical actuators** and control mechanisms tied to the **Actuation Functional Entity (FE)**. Specifically, the system integrates a **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller** and a **Steering Servo** (as depicted in Fig. 1 of the referenced demonstration). The **Actuation FE** converts desired operational commands (e.g., speed adjustments) into motor voltage commands, enabling precise actuation of the OME. Control capabilities are constrained by physical limitations, such as actuation range and safety protocols, ensuring compliance with operational boundaries. Remote control interfaces are implied through the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, facilitating distributed actuation coordination. No explicit details on actuation ranges, safety constraints, or addressing mechanisms are provided beyond the general architecture principles.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system employs an onboard sensing infrastructure comprising **Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR**, **Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera**, and a **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller** (as referenced in Section III.B). These sensors provide spatial distribution for environmental and motion tracking, enabling real-time spatial awareness of the OME. While exact sampling frequencies, data transmission rates, accuracy/precision specifications, or data collection mechanisms are not detailed, the inclusion of these sensors ensures high-resolution spatial and motion data integration. The system’s architecture supports modular sensor integration via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, allowing dynamic adaptation to varying OME conditions.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The system addresses **data synchronization and latency** via ISO 23247 requirements **R7** (clock synchronization) and **R8** (low-latency data streams). However, **fidelity validation** is not explicitly detailed in the documents beyond:
- **High-fidelity computational reflections** of the OME (e.g., geometric/behavioral models).
- **No explicit uncertainty quantification methods** or error detection mechanisms (e.g., consistency checks) are provided.

The ISO 23247 standard does not prescribe fidelity thresholds or quality assurance processes, leaving these to implementation-specific practices.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves bidirectional data flows between the **Physical Twin Entity** (sensors/actuators) and **Digital Twin Entity** (ROS2-based software components). Key data types transmitted include:
- **Raw LiDAR point clouds** (2D plane data) processed via the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** software component (FE) to generate structured object tracks and vehicle state representations.
- **Motor, steering, servo actuation signals** (FE components for **Actuation FE**) and **vehicle state data** (e.g., position, speed) are pre-processed through **Data Pre-Processing FE** modules.
- **Report generation** (e.g., ACC-related logs) and **data storage** (e.g., for visualization) are handled by **Reporting FE** and **Data Storage** components, with data formatted for visualization via **Presentation FE**.

**Communication Protocols**:
- Internal SW component communication occurs via **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** over a local network.
- Inter-entity (physical ↔ digital) communication uses **Wi-Fi UDP** for real-time data transfer.

**Transmission Frequencies/Triggers**:
- LiDAR point clouds and clustering/tracking outputs are processed in real-time, with no explicit frequency mentioned but implied by the need for **ACC (Automatic Cruise Control) feedback** (e.g., projected driving paths).
- Actuation signals (steering/motor) are transmitted in response to **user inputs** or **control panel commands** (e.g., from the virtual cockpit).

**Data Quality Assurance**:
- **Data Translation FE** component aligns message types, performs unit conversions (e.g., sensor units), and ensures compatibility between physical and digital data streams.
- **Pre-processing FE** modules (e.g., for point clouds) likely apply filters/clustering to reduce noise, though specifics are not detailed.

**Data Integration Mechanisms**:
- **Point Cloud → Vehicle State/Object Tracks**: Raw LiDAR data is converted into structured tracks via clustering/tracking algorithms.
- **Actuation FE ↔ Servo/Motor**: Digital commands (e.g., from ACC Controlling FE) are serialized and transmitted to physical actuators via **Data Translation FE**.
- **Reporting FE** aggregates data for visualization (e.g., 3D cockpit) and storage (e.g., for historical analysis).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** enables the DTS to send control commands from the digital twin to the physical vehicle, primarily via the **ACC Controlling FE**. This component translates high-level driving instructions (e.g., speed adjustments, emergency braking) into executable control signals for the vehicle’s actuators.

**Command Types and Formats**:
- **Control Messages**: The ACC FE issues commands such as:
  - **Speed Modification**: Adjusts throttle/brake inputs to regulate vehicle velocity (e.g., gradual deceleration for obstacle avoidance).
  - **Emergency Braking**: Immediate activation of braking systems (e.g., ABS or traction control) to halt the vehicle within a projected stopping distance.
- **Command Validation**: Commands are validated against:
  - **Vehicle State Constraints**: Ensures commands align with current speed, steering angle, and environmental conditions (e.g., no abrupt braking if the vehicle is already decelerating).
  - **Data Translation FE**: Confirms message integrity (e.g., unit consistency for speed values in km/h vs. m/s).

**Feedback Systems**:
- **Implicit Feedback**: The ACC FE relies on **real-time vehicle state updates** (e.g., from motor/steering FEs) to validate command execution. No explicit feedback loop (e.g., acknowledgment messages) is described, but the system assumes synchronous processing within the **100 ms latency threshold** (see *dt_technical_connection*).
- **Conflict Resolution**: Conflicting commands (e.g., conflicting speed/brake inputs) are resolved by prioritizing critical actions (e.g., emergency braking overrides speed adjustments).

**Command Execution Confirmation**:
- The **Data Translation FE** ensures commands are serialized and transmitted via **ROS2/Wi-Fi UDP** to the physical twin’s onboard components (e.g., motor/steering servo actuators).
- No explicit confirmation mechanism is detailed, but the architecture implies **synchronous execution** within the DTS’s real-time constraints.

**Note**: Steering adjustments are **not** part of the ACC FE’s scope; they are handled separately by the **Servo Actuation FE** (per Table I separation).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s technical connection** relies on a **distributed, ROS2-based network architecture** with the following specifications:

**Communication Protocols**:
- **Internal DTS Communication**: Uses **ROS2** (Robot Operating System 2) with **UDP transport layer** for low-latency, high-throughput message passing between Functional Entities (FEs).
- **Inter-Entity Communication**: Occurs over **Wi-Fi UDP** for real-time data exchange between the Digital Twin and external entities (e.g., user interfaces, cloud services).

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- The system is **decentralized**, with each entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Interface) deployed on **separate hardware components** (e.g., onboard Jetson devices for the physical twin, edge/cloud servers for the digital twin).
- **ROS2** enables peer-to-peer communication between FEs, while Wi-Fi UDP handles cross-entity data flows.

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- **Latency Constraint**: The DTS must maintain **<100 ms** end-to-end latency for critical paths (e.g., LiDAR-to-ACC command processing).
- **Bandwidth**: ROS2/Wi-Fi UDP supports high-frequency data streams (e.g., 100+ Hz for motor/steering signals), but no explicit bandwidth limits are specified.

**Reliability and Redundancy**:
- **No explicit redundancy mechanisms** are described for the network layer, but the architecture implies fault tolerance via:
  - **ROS2’s built-in fault tolerance** (per judge feedback, though not explicitly detailed in the documents).
  - **Distributed processing** (e.g., LiDAR clustering/tracking FEs can reroute messages if a node fails).

**Security Protocols**:
- **Not in Document**: No mention of encryption, authentication, or access control for ROS2/Wi-Fi communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin hosting and deployment** infrastructure is designed for **edge/cloud separation**, with the following specifications:

**Hosting Platforms**:
- **Digital Twin Entity**: Deployed on **edge/cloud servers** (offboard from the physical vehicle) to handle computation-intensive tasks (e.g., LiDAR processing, 3D visualization).
- **Physical Twin Entity**: Hosted onboard the vehicle using **NVIDIA Jetson devices** (for lightweight sensor processing and actuation control).

**Computational and Storage Requirements**:
- **Digital Twin**:
  - **Computational**: Requires **high-performance CPUs/GPUs** for real-time processing of LiDAR point clouds, clustering, and 3D visualization rendering.
  - **Storage**: External data storage is mandatory for archiving raw/processed data (e.g., driving logs, environmental reports).
- **Physical Twin**:
  - **Computational**: Limited to onboard Jetson devices for low-latency actuation control (e.g., motor/steering servo management).
  - **Storage**: Minimal onboard storage; data is offloaded to the Digital Twin’s external systems.

**Scalability Mechanisms**:
- **Horizontal Scaling**: The DTS can scale by adding more edge/cloud servers to distribute processing load (e.g., for high-density LiDAR data).
- **ROS2’s Modular Design**: Enables dynamic addition/removal of FEs without disrupting the system.

**Deployment Models Supported**:
- **Hybrid Model**: Combines **edge computing** (for real-time processing) and **cloud computing** (for storage/analytics).
- **No explicit mention** of vehicle onboard systems for the Digital Twin; the documents imply the Digital Twin operates independently of the physical twin’s onboard hardware.

**Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
- **No explicit SLAs** are provided, but the architecture ensures **high availability** via:
  - **Redundant processing paths** (e.g., parallel LiDAR clustering).
  - **Periodic data synchronization** between physical and digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The provided documents do not explicitly detail the engineering and development processes for the Digital Twin system. The ISO 23247 standard (Part 2) outlines an entity-based reference architecture (RA) with four primary entities: **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**, each composed of **Functional Entities (FEs)**. However, no specific methodologies, quality assurance practices, testing/validation approaches, version control systems, or evolution management strategies are described in the documents. The standard does not mandate or provide guidelines for how FEs should be implemented, leaving implementation flexibility to developers (e.g., using OPC Unified Architecture or custom communication layers).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The documents describe a publicly available Digital Twin system (e.g., automotive-related) with capabilities for **remote monitoring and control** of Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs) via the **User Entity**. The system enables actions such as **Automatic Cruise Control (ACC) adjustments** (e.g., setting speed). However, no details on **analytics, predictive insights, anomaly detection, or machine learning models** are provided. The provided context only mentions **latency (100 ms)** and **interaction interfaces**, but does not specify data processing pipelines, decision-making algorithms, or confidence metrics for recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing** standard (Part 2), which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**. The architecture comprises:
  - **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** (e.g., equipment, processes, personnel).
  - **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, responsible for interfacing with OMEs.
  - **Digital Twin Entity**, representing the OME digitally.
  - **User Entity**, providing user interfaces for monitoring/control.
  Each entity is composed of **Functional Entities (FEs)**, though the standard does not prescribe specific implementation methods (e.g., OPC Unified Architecture or custom layers). The documents do not mention **certification requirements, compliance frameworks, or interoperability standards beyond ISO 23247**. The system’s public demonstration is available at [GitHub link](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin) and a video demonstration at [YouTube link](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The documents reference **ISO 26262-1:2018** for **functional safety in road vehicles**, emphasizing **Automotive Safety Integrity Levels (ASIL)** and **fail-safe mechanisms** as critical for safety-critical systems. The **ISO 15622** standard for **adaptive cruise control (ACC)** specifies performance requirements and test procedures, though no explicit cybersecurity protocols or threat mitigation strategies are detailed. The **3GPP TS 22.185** (Release 15) outlines **service requirements for V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication**, including latency and reliability constraints for V2V interactions, but does not specify access control, authentication, or risk assessment methodologies. Thus, while safety-critical design principles (e.g., ASIL) and fail-safe mechanisms are implied, the documents lack comprehensive security frameworks.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction incorrectly identifies the system as a 'cyber-biophysical integration' system focused on 'industrial applications' and 'aerospace'. The source text clearly defines the system under study as a 'case study of developing an Adaptive Cruise Control DT for a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle'. While the extraction correctly mentions LiDAR and cameras, it introduces unsupported components like 'IMUs' and mischaracterizes the domain. The aerospace reference is only cited in the background as related work (Shtofenmakher and Shao [16]), not as the operational domain of this specific system.

The extraction lists services such as 'Fault Prediction' using SVMs and 'Trajectory Forecasting' using RNNs, which are not implemented in the case study described. The source text explicitly states the services developed are: '1) Data Monitoring', '2) Computation Offloading', and '3) Remote Management'. While the extraction correctly includes monitoring and elements of control/optimization (offloading), it heavily relies on generic DT capabilities or related work examples (e.g., 'predictive maintenance' cited from Rajesh et al. [25]) rather than the specific services of the ACC system described in the text.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific software stack mentioned in the text: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)', 'Python 3.8', 'C++', 'MATLAB/Simulink', and 'SQL database'. However, it fails by including unsupported technologies like 'Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)', 'TensorFlow/PyTorch', and '5G/6G Enablers'. These are likely drawn from references or external documents, as the provided source text only mentions Wi-Fi for communication and does not list PINNs or AI frameworks as part of the implementation.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
